title: How To Write a CV That Gets Interviews
excerpt: A simple structure for writing a clear, results-focused CV recruiters can scan in under 30 seconds.
author: CareerHub Team
content: Most recruiters scan a CV quickly, so clarity is more valuable than creativity. Keep your summary short and focused on your strongest value.
Use measurable achievements in each experience bullet. Instead of writing 'worked on projects', write 'delivered 3 projects that reduced support tickets by 25%'.
For each role, include technologies, ownership, and impact. This makes your profile easier to match against job descriptions.
Keep formatting consistent and avoid dense paragraphs. A clean one-page CV for early-career candidates is usually enough.

title: Remote Job Interview Preparation Checklist
excerpt: A practical checklist to prepare your setup, answers, and examples before a remote interview.
author: CareerHub Team
content: Treat a remote interview like an on-site interview. Test your camera, microphone, internet, and meeting link at least one day before.
Prepare short stories using the STAR method: situation, task, action, result. Keep one story each for leadership, conflict, and problem solving.
Keep your portfolio, resume, and notes open in separate tabs. This helps you answer quickly without losing flow.
End the interview with role-specific questions. Ask about success metrics for the first 90 days and team collaboration style.

title: Top Skills Hiring Managers Look For in 2026
excerpt: Beyond technical tools, these communication and execution skills help candidates stand out.
author: CareerHub Team
content: Technical skills open the door, but communication and ownership usually decide final selection. Companies want people who can turn requirements into outcomes.
Problem framing is a high-value skill. Strong candidates clarify assumptions before implementation and surface trade-offs early.
Written communication matters more in distributed teams. Clear updates, concise documentation, and status reporting build trust quickly.
Continuous learning is now expected. Show how you stay current through projects, courses, and practical experimentation.

title: How To Follow Up After an Interview
excerpt: A follow-up strategy that is polite, professional, and increases your chance of moving forward.
author: CareerHub Team
content: Send a concise thank-you message within 24 hours. Mention one specific discussion point from the interview to make it personal.
If you do not hear back by the expected timeline, send a short follow-up message. Keep it professional and avoid repeated messages.
Reinforce your fit by linking your experience to one challenge discussed in the interview.
Even if the result is negative, respond with appreciation. Professional follow-up helps your long-term network.
